title: Star Wars (radio)
content:

'''''Star Wars''''' is a thirteen-part radio adaptation of [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|the original ''Star Wars'' film]]. It is the first of the [[Star Wars radio dramas|''Star Wars'' radio dramatizations]] adapting the [[original trilogy]]. It was produced in [[1981]] by [[National Public Radio]] as part of its program ''[[wikipedia:NPR Playhouse|NPR Playhouse]]''.<ref name="ActuallyWorked">{{WebCite|url=https://www.npr.org/2015/12/18/460269884/that-time-npr-turned-star-wars-into-a-radio-drama-and-it-actually-worked|text=That Time NPR Turned 'Star Wars' Into A Radio Drama — And It Actually Worked|author=Derek John|date=2015-12-18|work=[[Wikipedia:NPR|NPR]]|archivedate=20220504024445}}</ref> [[Richard Toscan]] conceived the idea of a radio adaptation of ''Star Wars'' and served as executive producer. The series was made with the full cooperation of [[George Lucas]], who donated the rights and allowed the use of sound effects and music from the films.<ref name="Robb">{{BookCite|author=Brian J. Robb|title=A Brief Guide to Star Wars|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Running Press|Running Press]]|date=2012|isbn=9781780335834}}</ref>  [[Brian Daley]] adapted the film into a radio script, [[John Madden]] directed the voice actors, and [[Tom Voegeli]] mixed the dialogue, sound effects, and music.<ref name="Dramatization" /> Most of the movie actors were unavailable to reprise their roles ([[Harrison Ford]], for instance, was committed to [[Wikipedia:Raiders of the Lost Ark|''Raiders of the Lost Ark'']]), but [[Mark Hamill]] and [[Anthony Daniels]] returned to reprise [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] and [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]], respectively.<ref name="Robb" />

Following the success of ''Star Wars'' on the radio, two more radio dramatizations were produced: [[The Empire Strikes Back (radio)|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] ([[1983]]) and [[Return of the Jedi (radio)|''Return of the Jedi'']] ([[1996]]). The recordings of all three dramas were published by [[HighBridge Audio]].

==Plot summary==
The radio drama follows the plot of the film ''A New Hope,'' telling the story of [[Leia Skywalker Organa Solo|Princess Leia's]] capture by [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]], [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker's]] unexpected involvement in the 
[[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebellion]] against the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Empire]], the rescue of the princess, and the destruction of the [[DS-1 Orbital Battle Station/Legends|Death Star]]. The story is adapted to the auditory medium of radio. Additional dialogue describes action which in the films is conveyed visually. Radio has a less demanding narrative pace than cinema, which gave Daley the freedom to spend time developing characters and exploring events taking place before the first film.

The first two episodes - "[[A Wind to Shake the Stars]]" and "[[Points of Origin]]" - consist entirely of new material, while episode three - "[[Black Knight, White Princess, and Pawns]]" - begins with new material and transitions into an adaptation of the film's opening scene. ''Star Wars'' on the radio introduced a number of characters and concepts that would be referenced in later [[Star Wars Legends|Legends]] stories, such as a [[T-16 skyhopper/Legends|skyhopper]] race through [[Beggar's Canyon/Legends|Beggar's Canyon]], the [[Subjugation of Ralltiir]], and the [[Battle of Toprawa]]. It also adds detail to scenes only hinted at in the film. For example, ''A New Hope'' shows Threepio and Artoo emerging from a locked door in [[Mos Eisley/Legends|Mos Eisley]]; the radio drama shows how they enter the building by posing as workers for a [[Skywalker Technical Maintenance Service|made-up maintenance business]]. In another notable example, while the film implies that Vader tortures Leia, the radio episode "[[Death Star's Transit]]" depicts it explicitly.<ref name="Film">{{Film|IV}}</ref>

==Development==
===Conception===
NPR was looking for ways to expand its audience. The network was in financial trouble and was worried about even more serious cuts in the future: public broadcasting was one of the areas where presidential candidate [[wikipedia:Ronald Reagan|Ronald Reagan]] was calling for budget cuts. NPR's president [[wikipedia:Frank Mankiewicz|Frank Mankiewicz]] believed that reviving the genre of radio drama could draw new listeners.<ref name="Brandt">{{BookCite|author=Jessica K. Brandt |container=An Elegant Weapon for a More Civilized Age: Star Wars, Public Radio, and Middlebrow Cold War Culture. |title=A Galaxy Here and Now: Historical and Cultural Readings of Star Wars |contributor=edited by Peter W. Lee |publisher=McFarland |date=2016 |isbn=9781476662206}}</ref> Toscan, a theatre professor at the University of Southern California, had produced other dramas on KUSC, an NPR-affiliated station run by the university, for the program ''NPR Playhouse''; he credits a student of his with suggesting that he produce an adaptation of ''Star Wars''.<ref name="ArtsWatch">{{WebCite|url=https://www.orartswatch.org/interview-richard-toscan-champion-of-contemporary-audio-theater/ |text=Richard Toscan, champion of contemporary audio theater |author=Brett Campbell |work= Oregon ArtsWatch | date=2022-01-13 |archivedate=20220705213936 }}</ref> Toscan believed that this could "create a scandal" - something outrageous that would capture people's attention. ''Star Wars'', famous for its lavish visual effects, might seem impossible to adapt to the radio.<ref name="ActuallyWorked" /> Yet it also had rich, innovative, recognizable sound design and music that would lend themselves to a radio adaptation.<ref name="Makingof">''[[The Making of Star Wars For Radio: A Fable For the Mind's Eye]]''</ref>

KUSC's connection to the university helped gain the support of [[George Lucas]], who was a graduate. He sold the movie's rights, along with access to its music and sound library, for one dollar.<ref name="Robb" /> An agreement was made to secure support and additional funding from the BBC. An experienced scriptwriter for BBC radio was at first contracted to write the script. But [[Carol Titelman]], the Lucasfilm director who was assigned to serve as the drama's executive producer alongside Toscan, felt that the first draft suffered from the writer's lack of familiarity with ''Star Wars.'' Instead she tapped [[Brian Daley]], who had no experience in radio but plenty of experience writing for ''Star Wars,'' having authored [[The Han Solo Trilogy (hardcover)|''The Han Solo Trilogy'']].<ref name="Dramatization" /> This contributed to the breakdown of the deal with the BBC, which backed out soon afterward.<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{BookCite|author=Christopher H. Sterling|title=Encyclopedia of Radio|number=vol. 3|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Routledge|Routledge]]|date=2004|isbn=9781135456498|link=https://books.google.com/books?id=-UeveLMilioC&dq=Frank%20Mankiewicz%20star%20wars&pg=PA2206 }}</ref> Toscan's role was now to make sure that Daley's script followed an approach necessary for radio, using dialogue and sound to replace the film's visual input.<ref name="ArtsWatch" />

===Production===
Daley wrote the script while staying in North Hollywood between December 1979 and March 1980.<ref name="Dramatization" /> [[John Madden]] was hired as director that May. [[Mel Sahr]] cast the voice actors during that same period. Recording began in late June at Westlake Audio Studios in Los Angeles.<ref name="Encyclopedia" />

===Sound design===
One of the strengths of radio is that listeners are called upon to use their imaginations and create their own visuals mentally. The drama succeeds by the auditory associations with the movies, drawing on the subliminal power of [[John Williams]]'s incidental music, [[Ben Burtt]]'s sound design, and of course the voices of the leading actors from the cinema screen, [[Mark Hamill]] and [[Anthony Daniels]]. Director [[John Madden]] said, "Anyone who's ever listened to radio drama will testify to the fact that a play you hear will (remain) in your mind—twelve years later you'll remember it vividly. And the reason you'll remember it vividly is because you've done the work... it lives in your imagination."<ref name="Makingof" />

In Minnesota, [[Tom Voegeli]] worked long hours to mix the recorded dialogue with Burtt's effects and Williams's score.<ref name="Makingof" /> Working with [[wikipedia:Reel-to-reel audio tape recording|reel-to-reel audio tape]] and pre-digital tools such as a razor blade, Voegli could spend hours mixing only a few minutes of run time.<ref name="ActuallyWorked" />

===Promotion===
NPR launched an extensive campaign to promote the radio series. The tagline was, "You may think you've seen the movie; wait 'til you hear it!"<ref name="Makingof" /> NPR commissioned an original poster designed by [[Celia Strain]] depicting [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]] wearing headphones standing in front of an old-fashioned microphone, linking images from the film to images from the golden age of the radio drama.<ref name="Brandt" /> The series was launched at a special event at [[wikipedia:Griffith Observatory|Griffith Observatory]] in Los Angeles.<ref name="Robb" />The launch of the series was considered a resounding success, setting records for total NPR listeners, young listeners, and engagement in the form of calls and letters.<ref name="Dramatization" />

==Release and reception==
===Broadcasts===
The first episode, [[A Wind to Shake the Stars]], was first broadcast on [[March 2]], [[1981]], and weekly on Mondays for a total of thirteen weeks, ending on [[May 25]].<ref name="Kansas">{{WebCite|url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/the-kansas-city-star-star-wars-to-blas/77114939/ |text='Star Wars' to blast off as a radio series |author=Gerald B. Jordan |work=The Kansas City Star |date=1981-03-02 |archivedate=20240124044230}}</ref> Some local NPR affiliate stations aired the episodes on different days of the week. A half-hour documentary program, ''[[The Making of Star Wars For Radio: A Fable For the Mind's Eye]],'' first aired in February to promote the upcoming series; some stations chose instead to air it the week after the final episode.<ref name="Brandt" />

===Recordings===
All three series were released on cassette and CD in the US by [[HighBridge Audio]]; the first version retained NPR's cuts. In 1996 HighBridge released a Collector's Limited Edition of [[Star Wars radio dramas|the entire radio trilogy]] that restored the removed dialogue.<ref name="Dramatization" />

In [[October]] [[2013]], HighBridge Audio released two limited-edition, [[Wikipedia:MP3|mp3]] Collector's Volumes: ''Star Wars: A New Hope'' - The Original Radio Drama, "Light Side" and "Dark Side." Each volume includes the original ''Star Wars: A New Hope'' radio drama, an exclusive trading card from [[Topps]], and rare audio content and interviews from the making of the radio drama.<ref name="HighBridge">{{WebCite|url=http://starwarsradiodrama.com|text=The Original Radio Drama|work=starwarsradiodrama.com|archivedate=20131114213248|nolive=1}}</ref>

===Script===
In [[1994]] the entire radio script was published as a book called ''[[Star Wars: The National Public Radio Dramatization]]''. Brian Daley's introduction to the book recounted aspects of the drama's production.<ref name="Dramatization" /> The script of the first episode, "[[A Wind to Shake the Stars]]," was republished in an [[A Book Excerpt|article of the same name]] in [[Star Wars Galaxy Magazine 2|''Star Wars Galaxy Magazine'' 2]] together with illustrations and an interview with Daley.

==Continuity==
All radio scenes that are not featured in the original film are considered to be part of the [[Star Wars Legends|Legends]] continuity, and many have been referenced in other Legends stories. The [[1999]] one-shot comic book "[[Luke Skywalker's Walkabout]]" depicts a story that Luke tells about his childhood in the radio episode "[[Jedi that Was, Jedi to Be]]."  The [[2003]] two-part comic arc ''[[Star Wars: Empire: Princess... Warrior|Princess... Warrior]]'' adapts a portion of the episode "Points of Origin". The [[2013]] trading card set [[2013 Topps Star Wars Illustrated: A New Hope|''Star Wars'' Illustrated: ''A New Hope'']] depicts many scenes from the drama that were not in the film.

One scene in the seventh episode, "[[The Han Solo Solution]]," is considered [[canon|non-canon]] because it was later contradicted by the film. Like several other parts of the radio drama, it adapted a scene deleted from the original film ''[[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|Star Wars]]''. In the deleted scene, Han is confronted by [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] and his gang. Daley's adaptation replaces Jabba with his henchman, [[Heater (henchman)|Heater]]. The [[1997]] [[The Star Wars Trilogy Special Edition|Episode IV Special Edition]] restored this scene, adding Jabba as he appeared in ''[[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|Return of the Jedi]]'', relegating the radio scene with Heater to non-canon.<ref name="SWArchive-A New Hope: Special Edition - What has changed?">{{SWArchive|url=episode-iv/bts/article/f19970115/index.html|text=''A New Hope'': Special Edition - What has changed?}}</ref> The existence of a henchman named Heater remains part of the Legends continuity because he is mentioned in dialogue in the previous episode, "[[The Millennium Falcon Deal]]."

Some elements from the radio drama have been incorporated into the current ''Star Wars'' [[canon]]. Elements from Luke and Leia's backstories are retold in the [[2015]] novel [[A New Hope: The Princess, the Scoundrel, and the Farm Boy|''The Princess, the Scoundrel, and the Farm Boy'']].<ref name="Bracken">{{Twitter|alexbracken|status/636708724061696000|[[Alexandra Bracken]]|quote=I was lucky enough to be allowed to adapt from the very cool ANH radio drama for The Princess, The Scoundrel, and The Farmboy|archivefile=File:Bracken Radio Tweet.png}}</ref> Artoo-Detoo's sabotage of [[R5-D4]] was first introduced in the radio episode "[[While Giants Mark Time]]" and features in the canon story "[[The Red One]]," written from R5's perspective. The story "[[Verge of Greatness]]" incorporates a subplot from the radio drama centering on [[Conan Antonio Motti|Admiral Motti]] and [[Wilhuff Tarkin|Grand Moff Tarkin]], using some of the exact dialogue from the radio episodes "Death Star's Transit" and "[[Force and Counterforce]]." Both of these stories were published in the [[2017]] anthology ''[[From a Certain Point of View]].''<ref name="From a Certain Point of View">''[[From a Certain Point of View]]''</ref>
